web.markdown:
![Thumbnail (1920x1080)](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/tNI8IYr-PIc/maxresdefault.jpg)
# [JavaFX and Scene Builder - Make Components Draggable](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tNI8IYr-PIc)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [Random code](https://www.youtube.com/@Randomcode_0)
**Uploaded at**: 2021-08-16
**Published at**: 
**Length**: 03:43
**Views**: 11704
**Likes**: 162
**Category**: Science & Technology

## Description

```
I made a small setup that allows us to quickly turn any JavaFX component draggable. 

My channel publishes videos regarding programming, software engineering especially Java and JavaFX. If that is what you are looking for then do not hesitate to join me on this journey!

Source code: https://gist.github.com/Da9el00/74cc0100b67ade75308f3875c2c681cb

Subscribe to my YouTube channel:
https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCyjEXAHK8xfRmlnkat2ympQ?sub_confirmation=1
```

## Transcript

hey it's suffering student here and
today i'm going to showcase how we can
make java effects components draggable
so first on my window i have an angle
pane with some buttons radio button and
a text area
and i can then drag my anchor pane
around
i also have a shape in this case a
rectangle which is also draggable
and as you can see wherever i click on
my components and drag
i keep dragging from this point of
click
so let's have a look at the setup so
inside my controller i just very simply
have my rectangle and my anchor pane i
then have a draggable maker class which
the class i made that contains all the
draggable components and how we make
stuff draggable
just make an object of my draggable
maker class i then simply have a method
in my draggable maker class that is
named make draggable where i can call
this on
a component that is a subclass of the
superclass in javafx node which kind of
like the main class in javafx everything
else inherits
so for example i have my rectangle or my
anchor pane in this case so i just make
them draggable
and
that make them draggable but how it
actually works if we look at the make
draggable method inside draggable maker
i have two attributes a private double
which is kind of like the mouse angle of
x and y so where i click on my component
so i can keep dragging up from this spot
and then just set to listeners
on them set on mouse pressed and set on
mouse dragged so whenever i press on one
of my components
let's see i click now
i then save
where on my
in this case rectangle i've clicked and
it is saved in this
variables mouse angle x and mouse anchor
y
whenever i start dragging
my component in this case my rectangle
is then moved that layout x and move it
to the position off where the mouse
event that gets seen x so where my mouse
is inside the scene
subtracted by the starting position
so if i remove the starting position of
the mouse i would only be able to drag
from the left top
corner but
this allows me to drag it from anywhere
and drag it
any way i want as well
and using a setup using a class using
the oop object oriented programming
setup
with classes makes it very easy and very
fast let's say inside my scene builder
where you can see i have my two
components as well let's see i wanted to
add another rectangle
up here for example
and i just gave an eye id
or rectangle two
i know barrick for aiding
and
just get this information into my
controller
i can then just very simply
hold mine my draggable maker dot make
draggable on rectangle 2
and now when i run the program
my new rectangle is also
draggable
so as you can see using the setup and
using a class to contain all the
setup for draggable utilities it makes
it quite easy to very quickly make a lot
of stuff draggable if that's what you
want so
this was my showcase of how i would make
stuff draggable in javafx if you enjoyed
this video please leave a like and
subscribe and wish all wonderful day

[JavaFX Concurrency](https://jenkov.com/tutorials/javafx/concurrency.html) # JavaFX Drag and Drop - [Drop Source and Drop Target](https://jenkov.com/tutorials/javafx/drag-and-drop.html#drop-source-and-drop-target) - [JavaFX Drag and Drop Example](https://jenkov.com/tutorials/javafx/drag-and-drop.html#javafx-drag-and-drop-example) | | | | --- | --- | | ![Jakob Jenkov]() | Jakob Jenkov
Last update: 2020-11-12

[![Follow on Twitter]()](https://twitter.com/#!/jjenkov "Follow on Twitter")[![Connect on LinkedIn]()](http://www.linkedin.com/pub/jakob-jenkov/0/a8/4a3 "Connect on LinkedIn")[![Subscribe on YouTube]()](https://www.youtube.com/user/jjenkov "Subscribe on YouTube")[![Subscribe to RSS Feed]()](http://jenkov.com/rss.xml "Subscribe to RSS Feed")[![Subscribe to Telegram Channel]()](https://t.me/jenkov_com "Subscribe to Telegram Channel") | The _JavaFX_ _Drag and Drop_ support enables you to design your JavaFX applications so that users can drag JavaFX nodes (controls) onto other JavaFX nodes and have your application be able to listen for, and react to these drag-and-drop events. Drag and drop can be useful as a way for users to specify actions more easily, in the situations where that makes sense - e.g dragging files to new locations, or data to analytics operations etc. By the way, the JavaFX Drag and Drop support is quite similar to the [HTML5 Drag and Drop support](https://jenkov.com/html5/drag-and-drop.html). If you already know HTML5 drag and drop functionality (via JavaScript) it will be easier to understand how JavaFX Drag and Drop functionality works. If not - just start directly with JavaFX Drag and Drop support. ## Drop Source and Drop Target The JavaFX Drag and Drop support divides JavaFX controls involved in drag and drop into the categories _drag sources_ and _drop targets_ . A drag source is a JavaFX control (e.g. a [2D shape](https://jenkov.com/tutorials/javafx/2d.html), [ImageView](https://jenkov.com/tutorials/javafx/imageview.html) etc.) which can be dragged onto another node. In general, most JavaFX controls can be used as drag sources. A drop target is a JavaFX control onto which you can drag and then drop a drag source. When the drag source is dropped onto the drop target an event is fired which your application can listen for and respond to. ## JavaFX Drag and Drop Example Here is a JavaFX drag and drop example showing how to make one 2D circle into a drag source, and another 2D circle into a drop target: ``` import javafx.application.Application; import javafx.event.EventHandler; import javafx.scene.Scene; import javafx.scene.input.*; import javafx.scene.layout.Pane; import javafx.scene.paint.Color; import javafx.scene.shape.Circle; import javafx.stage.Stage; public class DragAndDropExample extends Application { public static void main(String[] args) { launch(args); } public void start(Stage primaryStage) { Circle circle = createCircle("#ff00ff", "#ff88ff",100); circle.setOnDragDetected((MouseEvent event) -> { System.out.println("Circle 1 drag detected"); Dragboard db = circle.startDragAndDrop(TransferMode.ANY); ClipboardContent content = new ClipboardContent(); content.putString("Circle source text"); db.setContent(content); }); circle.setOnMouseDragged((MouseEvent event) -> { event.setDragDetect(true); }); Circle circle2 = createCircle("#00ffff", "#88ffff",300); circle2.setOnDragOver(new EventHandler() { public void handle(DragEvent event) { if (event.getGestureSource() != circle2 && event.getDragboard().hasString()) { event.acceptTransferModes(TransferMode.COPY_OR_MOVE); } event.consume(); } }); circle2.setOnDragDropped((DragEvent event) -> { Dragboard db = event.getDragboard(); if (db.hasString()) { System.out.println("Dropped: " + db.getString()); event.setDropCompleted(true); } else { event.setDropCompleted(false); } event.consume(); }); Pane pane = new Pane(); pane.getChildren().add(circle); pane.getChildren().add(circle2); Scene scene = new Scene(pane, 1024, 800, true); primaryStage.setScene(scene); primaryStage.setTitle("2D Example"); primaryStage.show(); } private Circle createCircle(String strokeColor, String fillColor, double x) { Circle circle = new Circle(); circle.setCenterX(x); circle.setCenterY(200); circle.setRadius(50); circle.setStroke(Color.valueOf(strokeColor)); circle.setStrokeWidth(5); circle.setFill(Color.valueOf(fillColor)); return circle; } } ``` Next: [JavaFX Concurrency](https://jenkov.com/tutorials/javafx/concurrency.html) | | | | --- | --- | | [Tweet](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=http://tutorials.jenkov.com/tutorials/javafx/drag-and-drop.html&original_referer=jjenkov) | | ![Jakob Jenkov]() | Jakob Jenkov

YouTube]()](https://www.youtube.com/user/jjenkov "Subscribe on YouTube")[![Subscribe to RSS Feed]()](http://jenkov.com/rss.xml "Subscribe to RSS Feed")[![Subscribe to Telegram metadata.author: Jakob Jenkov
metadata.twitter:creator: @jjenkov
metadata.ogDescription: The JavaFX Drag and Drop support enables your users to drag JavaFX nodes onto other JavaFX nodes and have that drag-and-drop action trigger drag-and-drop events which your application can listen for and react to.

web.title: Drag-and-Drop in JavaFX (Draggable Icons), Part 1 - Joel Graff
web.description: Part 1 of a series of posts on how to develop JavaFX custom controls that demonstrate using drag-and-drop to create and move objects.

metadata.ogDescription: Part 1 of a series of posts on how to develop JavaFX custom controls that demonstrate using drag-and-drop to create and move objects. The result is a simple application that mimics node-style edito…
metadata.description: Part 1 of a series of posts on how to develop JavaFX custom controls that demonstrate using drag-and-drop to create and move objects. The result is a simple application that mimics node-style editors found in major software like Blender and Unreal Development Kit (UDK).
